EO-01.mapping_to_concept: MAESTRO directly addresses this outcome through Pillar 1 (Neuro-Symbolic Planning and Reasoning) and Pillar 2 (Adaptive Memory Architecture). Pillar 1 develops a hybrid neuro-symbolic planning engine combining LLM-based reasoning with symbolic planning methods (Monte Carlo Tree Search, hierarchical task networks) to decompose high-level goals into executable sub-task graphs with verification checkpoints, plan monitoring, and self-correction — producing improvements in agent autonomy and robustness via structured planning mechanisms and reasoning capabilities. Pillar 2 develops a unified adaptive memory architecture integrating working memory, episodic memory, and semantic memory under a memory controller that manages long-term and short-term information flow — directly providing the memory management improvements the outcome specifies. project_summary.json abstract confirms: 'structured planning, persistent memory management, and robust multi-step reasoning to autonomously accomplish complex real-world tasks.' The concept frames the problem as agents being 'fundamentally brittle' and the design target as agents that are 'robust, trustworthy, and deployable in real-world settings' (concept_note.md, Vision section), addressing all three dimensions of the expected outcome: planning mechanisms, memory management, and reasoning capabilities.
EO-01.tier3_evidence_ref: concept_note.md — Vision section (robustness/autonomy framing); Pillar 1 Neuro-Symbolic Planning and Reasoning section (planning mechanisms and reasoning capabilities); Pillar 2 Adaptive Memory Architecture section (memory management); strategic_positioning.md — Positioning Against Expected Outcomes point 1 (explicit EO-01 mapping); project_summary.json — abstract field ('structured planning, persistent memory management, robust multi-step reasoning') and keywords ('planning and reasoning', 'memory-augmented AI')

EO-02.mapping_to_concept: MAESTRO directly addresses this outcome through Pillar 3 (Decentralised Multi-Agent Coordination). The concept defines a formal task-delegation protocol enabling agents to decompose tasks, negotiate subtask assignments, and establish shared commitment structures with provable convergence — directly constituting innovative protocols for effective decentralised coordination. Conflict resolution mechanisms address agent plan interference, contested resources, and inconsistent world models. The concept explicitly states 'Many real-world tasks exceed the scope of any single agent' (concept_note.md, Pillar 3), directly framing the outcome requirement for capabilities 'beyond the capabilities of individual agents.' Three demonstrators (healthcare clinical decision support, advanced manufacturing process optimisation, autonomous logistics planning) validate multi-agent coordination in real-world sectors with heterogeneous agents. The concept uses 'decentralised multi-agent coordination' as a core framing term (British-English spelling is the definitionally identical variant of 'decentralized coordination') and 'multi-agent coordination protocols' as the primary mechanism. strategic_positioning.md explicitly maps to this outcome under 'Positioning Against the Expected Outcomes, point 2.'
EO-02.tier3_evidence_ref: concept_note.md — Pillar 3 Decentralised Multi-Agent Coordination section (formal task-delegation protocol, conflict resolution, auditable interactions; explicit 'beyond single agent' framing); Validation and Demonstrators section (three multi-agent sector demonstrators); strategic_positioning.md — Positioning Against Expected Outcomes point 2 (explicit EO-02 mapping with differentiation rationale); project_summary.json — abstract ('decentralised multi-agent coordination protocols') and keywords ('multi-agent systems', 'AI agents')

scope_conflict_log.description: SR-05 (mandatory) requires proposals to 'incorporate qualitative and quantitative KPIs, benchmarking, and progress monitoring mechanisms to demonstrate progress.' The concept corpus does not explicitly define a KPI framework (qualitative or quantitative), does not commit to a systematic benchmarking methodology as a structured project element, and does not describe 'progress monitoring mechanisms' as a distinct methodological component. The term 'benchmarks' appears once in concept_note.md in the context of disseminated outputs ('frameworks, models, benchmarks, and demonstrator results') and 'evaluation benchmarks' appears once in strategic_positioning.md as a shared integration artefact — neither constitutes an explicit KPI and progress monitoring regime. The concept's validation approach is demonstrator-success-based (three sector demonstrators validated with industry partners), whereas SR-05 expects an explicit KPI-driven monitoring and benchmarking methodology as part of the proposal's methodology design. This is a framing mismatch: the call frames progress demonstration as a structured KPI/benchmarking regime; the concept frames it as demonstrator validation. The concept must be revised to explicitly introduce a KPI framework, quantitative and qualitative success indicators, and a benchmarking methodology aligned with SR-05.

SR-07 (mandatory) requires 'Activities must start at TRL 2 and achieve TRL 5 by the end of the project.' strategic_positioning.md confirms TRL 5 as the end-of-project target ('validated in operational environments at TRL 5'), satisfying the end-point condition. However, no concept document (concept_note.md, strategic_positioning.md, project_summary.json) explicitly declares TRL 2 as the starting technology readiness level. The concept's research approach — developing new neuro-symbolic architectures from research principles — is logically consistent with TRL 2, but this is an inference, not an explicit declaration. The mandatory TRL trajectory condition (start = TRL 2, end = TRL 5) is only half-declared. The concept must be revised to explicitly state TRL 2 as the starting level and articulate the TRL 2-to-TRL 5 progression trajectory to satisfy SR-07.

strategic_differentiation: MAESTRO's primary differentiator is the integrated co-design of three mutually reinforcing capabilities — neuro-symbolic planning and reasoning (Pillar 1), adaptive memory architecture (Pillar 2), and decentralised multi-agent coordination (Pillar 3) — into a unified architecture, in contrast to the state of the art in which these capabilities exist in three largely disconnected research communities (LLM-based agents; classical planning and multi-agent systems; memory-augmented neural systems). This integration is the project's stated primary differentiator: MAESTRO addresses all four call areas (planning, reasoning, memory management, multi-agent coordination) as an integrated research programme rather than independent work streams, with the planning engine using the memory architecture to maintain long-horizon coherence and the multi-agent protocol using both planning and memory to coordinate heterogeneous agents. A second differentiator is the neuro-symbolic approach: combining LLM flexibility with formal symbolic planning guarantees (verifiable sub-task decomposition, provable convergence, plan monitoring with recovery) produces architecturally distinct capabilities that differentiate MAESTRO from prompt-engineering-based agent frameworks. A third differentiator is the three-sector validation strategy across healthcare, advanced manufacturing, and autonomous logistics, co-designed with sector-leading industry partners, ensuring research advances are generalisable capabilities rather than domain-specific artefacts and positioning MAESTRO outputs for post-project exploitation through a technology transfer partner and the AI-on-demand platform.

decision_log_entry.tier_authority_applied: Tier 2B expected_outcomes.json (source: HORIZON-CL4-2026-05-DIGITAL-EMERGING-02.slice.json — 2 outcome entries, both status Confirmed in source); Tier 2B scope_requirements.json (sources: HORIZON-CL4-2026-05-DIGITAL-EMERGING-02.slice.json and HORIZON-CL4-2026-05-DIGITAL-EMERGING-02.json — 10 requirements evaluated of which 7 mandatory, 3 non-mandatory)
